policy_statement: Supply natural gas to hotels/resorts in Luxor's eastern bank, saving about 8,157 MWh/year of electricity and reducing GHG emissions by 2.8 tCO2eq/y (as stated).
mechanism_description: The action plans to supply natural gas to hotels and resorts, reducing their electricity consumption and associated greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Fuel switching (electricity → natural gas) → ↓ net fossil CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed human activity/system: Hotels and resorts change their energy supply for heating/cooking/thermal needs from electricity to piped natural gas (and potentially adjust equipment to gas-fired end-use).  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced demand for grid electricity (which is partially/mostly supplied by fossil generators) and/or improved conversion efficiency relative to electric resistance/inefficient electric systems; emissions shift to on-site gas combustion with typically lower CO₂ per unit useful heat than the displaced marginal electricity.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in CO₂e emissions (primarily CO₂, with some relevance of methane leakage depending on the gas supply chain).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced net greenhouse gas emissions from shifting energy service provision from electricity (often more carbon-intensive/less efficient) to direct natural gas combustion.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Fuel switching in building energy supply reduces net CO₂e by lowering grid electricity demand and substituting with (typically lower-carbon-per-useful-heat) natural gas.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there may be an energy-efficiency co-benefit (less primary energy per delivered heat) and operational cost savings, the defining climate-relevant objective/effect across policies using this mechanism is direct GHG emissions reduction—i.e., mitigation. It is not primarily about reducing climate hazard exposure (adaptation) or ecosystem carbon sinks (nature-based).  
3) Edge cases applied: If the electricity being displaced is already very low-carbon (high renewables/nuclear) or if upstream methane leakage is high, the net mitigation benefit could be small or even negative; however, the mechanism as stated is explicitly intended/claimed to reduce GHG emissions via reduced electricity use, so the primary category remains Mitigation.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is switching end-use energy from electricity to natural gas is fundamentally an emissions-impacting energy substitution and therefore falls under Mitigation as the primary category (even if magnitude varies by grid mix and leakage).  
5) Note on secondary subtype: Any secondary categorization (e.g., “energy efficiency co-benefit,” “cost savings,” “air quality impacts,” or “grid peak reduction”) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on specific evidence and context, and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If displaced electricity is low/zero-carbon or if gas supply-chain methane leakage is significant, net GHG reductions may be minimal or reversed; also, switching to gas can create lock-in of fossil infrastructure. Despite these, the dominant intended pathway is still emissions mitigation via fuel switching.
key_indicators: "supply natural gas to hotels/resorts"; "save 8,157 MWh/year of electricity"; "reduce GHG emissions"
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as an incentive-program or pricing-mechanism if the underlying plan relies on subsidies/discounted connection fees, but the verbatim text emphasizes the physical provision of gas supply rather than financial or regulatory levers.
